coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of amino-carbon dots/gold nanoclusters FRET ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing of Pb2+/Cu2+
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Li
Cao, Hai-Xia
He, Yu-Sheng
Pan, Chang-Gang
Sun, Ting-Kai
Zhang, Xin-Yang
Wang, Chuan-Yun
Liang, Guo-Xi
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric fluorescent probes could eliminate the influence from experimental factors and improve the detection accuracy. In this work, under mild conditions, through the electrostatic attraction between positively charged amino-carbon dots (N-CDots) and negatively charged gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), a novel Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) ratiometric fluorescent probe was fabricated for reliable, selective, and sensitive sensing of Pb2+ and Cu2+. The as-prepared probe shows dual emission peaks at 440 nm and 565 nm with efficient FRET under a single excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The red fluorescence of AuNCs is enhanced by Pb2+ and quenched by Cu2+, while the blue fluorescence of N-CDots stay stable, resulting in fluorescent ratiometric changes. With this strategy, the probe was successfully applied to detect Pb2+ or Cu2+ in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries at three spiking levels ranging from 94 to 104%. Moreover, it could realize the visual semi-quantitative determination of these two heavy metal ions by naked eyes. The good results imply that the developed N-CDots/AuNCs probe is conducive to Pb2+ or Cu2+ determination and possesses the great application potential for water-quality monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent
Carbon dots
Gold nanoclusters
FRET
Pb2+
Cu2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-CDots/AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe
Characterization of the as-prepared N-CDots, AuNCs and N-CDots/AuNCs
PL mechanism of the ratiometric nanoprobe
Pb2+-induced aggregation induced emission enhancement (AIEE) of AuNCs
Detection of Pb2+ and Cu2+ in river water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagrams showing (a) the preparation of N-CDots/AuNCs probe and (b) ratiometric fluorescent detection of Pb2+ and Cu2+ by the prepared probe.
TEM images of (a) N-CDots, (b) AuNCs, and (c) N-CDots/AuNCs. Inserts are high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and enlarged images.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDots, AuNCs and N-CDots/AuNCs probe, (b) UV–vis spectrum of AuNCs and fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CDots. Exicitation at 380 nm, (c) Zeta potentials of the nanoparticles.
The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of N-CDots, AuNCs alone and in N-CDots/AuNCs complex, respectively.
TEM images of N-CDots/AuNCs probe in the presence of (a) Pb2+ and (b) Cu2+. The concentrations of metal ions are all 20 μM.

(a, a’) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDots/AuNCs probe in the presence of different amounts of Pb2+ and Cu2+; (b, b’) Plots of fluorescence intensity ratio of I565/I440 versus the concentration of Pb2+ and Cu2+; (c, c’) Corresponding photographs of ratiometric probe containing various amount of Pb2+ and Cu2+ under a UV light.
The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements. Excitation at 380 nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric probe in the presence of Pb2+, Cu2+ and other metal ions; (b) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I565/I440) of the probe in the presence of various analytes, insert show the visual fluorescence color of the probe after addition of various analytes under a UV light. The concentrations of metal ions are all 20 μM. Excitation at 380 nm.
The fluorescence lifetimes and the FRET efficiency.
Comparison of several fluorescent probes for Pb2+ /Cu2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of amino-carbon dots/gold nanoclusters FRET ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing of Pb2+/Cu2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environment Science, School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ratiometric fluorescent probes could eliminate the influence from experimental factors and improve the detection accuracy. In this work, under mild conditions, through the electrostatic attraction between positively charged amino-carbon dots (N-CDots) and negatively charged gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), a novel Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) ratiometric fluorescent probe was fabricated for reliable, selective, and sensitive sensing of Pb2+ and Cu2+. The as-prepared probe shows dual emission peaks at 440 nm and 565 nm with efficient FRET under a single excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The red fluorescence of AuNCs is enhanced by Pb2+ and quenched by Cu2+, while the blue fluorescence of N-CDots stay stable, resulting in fluorescent ratiometric changes. With this strategy, the probe was successfully applied to detect Pb2+ or Cu2+ in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries at three spiking levels ranging from 94 to 104%. Moreover, it could realize the visual semi-quantitative determination of these two heavy metal ions by naked eyes. The good results imply that the developed N-CDots/AuNCs probe is conducive to Pb2+ or Cu2+ determination and possesses the great application potential for water-quality monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, numerous efforts have been made to develop the ratiometric fluorescence probes, which should provide a built-in correction for environmental effects by measurement the ratio of two fluorescent peaks [1]. The Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a widely adopted strategy in ratiometric fluorescence detection [2,3], in which an excited donor transfers energy to an acceptor [4]. The basic FRET principles require an overlapping between donor emission and acceptor absorption [5]. Thus, the choice of an effective donor/acceptor pair is an important issue for the fabrication of a FRET probe [6]. A number of ratiometric fluorescence probes using organic small molecules have been reported based on FRET strategy [7]. However, organic small molecule probes generally suffer from the complicated design and synthesis, and some of them are prone to photobleaching, which affects the stability of fluorescence emission [8].

Fluorescence nanomaterials such as carbon dots (CDots) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), could be ideal alternatives owing to their properties of easily synthesize and low-photobleaching [6,8]. Especially, the wavelength-tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties makes CDots particularly appealing for their use as FRET donor [9–11]. So far, several interesting works about CDots/AuNCs FRET probes have been reported. For example, Niu et al. [12] and Li et al. [13] have built FRET ratiometric fluorescent probes based on the covalent-binding of CDots and AuNCs for Cd2+ ions, l-ascorbic acid and H2O2 sensing. Nonetheless, these covalent-binding involves tough and time-consuming integrated work. In other words, notwithstanding these developments in the field of CDots/AuNCs FRET probes, there are numerous opportunities for new and facile different approaches in linking methodology. Xie et al. developed a novel nonconjugating method for the synthesis of dual-emission CDots/AuNCs probe by in situ formation of AuNCs on the surfaces of CDots. This method avoids the need of hazardous coupling reagent and the time-consuming procedures. Nevertheless, the production is not a FRET probe [14]. Very recently, Yu and co-workers designed a CDots/AuNCs FRET probe based on a supramolecular assembly strategy. Compared to traditional covalent connection, the supramolecular assembly could be used to regulate the fluorescence performance of FRET system more easily owing to its controllable, reversible and well-organized characteristics [15]. However, the facile synthesis of FRET probe is still scarce and requires investigation.

On the other hand, design and synthesis of chemosensors for heavy metal cations such as Pb2+ and Cu2+ with desirable properties have been of interest to chemists for many years because their potential toxicity, especially in environmental contexts [16]. A number of fluorescence sensors for Pb2+ and Cu2+ have been reported [17–19], most of which, however, are single wavelength intensity-based probes. A major limitation of this type probe is that the detection is influenced by the probe concentration, autofluorescence, and instrumental factors [4]. In contrast, ratiometric measurements could overcome these problems [20].

In this study, we describe a versatile and fast construction method for a FRET ratiometric fluorescent probe by simply mixing amino-riched CDots (N-CDots) and AuNCs. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the N-CDots with a large amount of amines groups on their surface, used as an energy donor and a reference signal, were prepared by a one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis; the glutathione capped AuNCs with a negatively charged surface, used as an energy acceptor and a reporter, were prepared by a one-pot synthesis method; N-CDots and AuNCs were directly connected by electrostatic interactions in aqueous solution without any other linker. Aside from describing a facile way to synthesize relatively inexpensive FRET probe, the present study also provides accurate ratiometric quantification and visual semi-quantitative determination of Pb2+ or Cu2+. Practicably, the probe shows a feature of certain selectivity for Pb2+ or Cu2+ in natural water and demonstrating the potential in environmental ratiometric analysis.

Ethylenediamine (EDA) and L-Glutathione in the reduced form (GSH) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), L-Arginine (Arg), Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Pb(NO3)2 and other metal salts Zn(Ac)2·2H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, KCl and NaCl were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). All of these reagents were of analytical grade and used without further treatment. Ultra-pure water was used in all experiment.

Morphologies of N-CDots, AuNCs, N-CDots/AuNCs were analyzed using a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100, JEOL). Zeta potentials of the nanoparticles were analyzed using a Zeta potential analyzer (ZetaPALS, Brookhaven). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a UV-VIS-NIR spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu). FT-IR spectra were recorded on a FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet 6700, Nicolet). The fluorescence measurements were carried out on a fluorescence spectrometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu) with the instrument settings as follows: λex = 380 nm (slit 5 nm), λem = 400–700 nm (slit 5 nm), PMT detector voltage = 600 V.

The amino-riched N-CDots with a positively charged surface were prepared by a microwave-assisted method [21]. Typically, 87.0 mg Arg, 33.0 μL EDA and 100.0 μL ultra-pure water were transferred into a microwave reactor, then the mixture heated to 240 °C and maintained 180 s under 26 bar and 200 W conditions. The stock solution finally turned brown, indicating the formation of N-CDots. Then, the resulted brown solution was diluted with 2 mL ultra-pure water. After filtered through a 0.45 μm microporous membrane, the deep yellow solution was dialyzed with a membrane (cut-off, 1 000 Da) for further purification. Then the obtained N-CDots solution was stored at 4 °C and 10-fold diluted before further usage.

The GSH-capping AuNCs were synthesized according to the procedure from a previously reported paper with minor modifications [22]. Brieﬂy, 1 mL HAuCl4 (10 mM), 1.5 mL GSH (10 mM), and 2.5 mL ultra-pure water were mixed firstly. This mixture gently stirred for 24 h at 70 °C and an aqueous solution of AuNCs was formed. Then the supernatant was harvested and dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (cut-off, 1 000 Da) after a 5-min centrifugation at 6 000 rpm. Finally, the dialyzed AuNCs solution was diluted with ultra-pure water to a constant volume of 10.0 mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CDots/AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CDots/AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe was facilely prepared by a simple mixing of the aqueous solution of above prepared N-CDots and AuNCs by the quality ratio of 1:10. The obtained probe was stored at 4 °C for further use.

100 μL of N-CDots/AuNCs probe, 100 μL of Pb2+ or Cu2+ standard solution at different concentrations were mixed firstly. After incubating at room temperature for 10 min, the fluorescence intensity was measured. The working curves were also plotted between the values of the intensity ratio (I565/I440) and the concentrations of Pb2+ or Cu2+.
Different metal ions including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Mg2+ (20 μM for each) were separately introduced into the prepared probe solution by the same way in order to investigated the selectivity.

For investigating the practicality, the natural river water samples were measured. The river water samples were obtained from Changjiang River (Zhenjiang, Jiangsu) and filtered using 0.45 μm microporous membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the as-prepared N-CDots, AuNCs and N-CDots/AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: First of all, the morphologies and the sizes of the nanomaterials were characterized by TEM, as shown in Fig. 1 . The as-synthesized N-CDots and AuNCs show uniform size and shape with diameters of about 5 nm and 2 nm, respectively (Fig. 1a and b). The images also imply that the materials are highly monodisperse. Well-resolved lattice fringes both of N-CDots and AuNCs are observed in high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images. Moreover, it can be seen that the particles tend to aggregate after forming the N-CDots/AuNCs probe (Fig. 1c).

The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1) reveals that N-CDots mainly contain carbon, nitrogen, oxygen. The absorptions at 1194 cm−1 can be attributed to COC bonds, while the absorptions at 1719 cm−1 confirm the presence of CO bonds. Moreover, a strong absorption peak at 3468 cm−1 indicates the presence of −OH. The symmetrical and antisymmetric peaks at 3200–3400 cm−1 clearly show the presence of NH2 functional groups. Furthermore, CN (1462 and 1401 cm−1) functional groups can be identified. These results indicate the existence of COOH and NH2 on the surface of N-CDots [21]. The FT-IR spectrum of AuNCs (Fig. S1) shows a peak at 3410 cm-1 which corresponds to the stretching vibration of OH, and a peak at 1646 cm−1 confirming the existence of COOH [23].

The UV–vis spectrum of N-CDots (Fig. S2) shows a strong absorption in the deep UV (<250 nm), which are assigned to the π-π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds according to previous reports [24,25]. An additional shoulder peak ∼290 nm is assigned to the n-π* electrons transition from the nonbonding orbital of the surface functional group to the π* orbital of the sp2 carbon domain [26–29]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of AuNCs (Fig. S2) exhibits absorption between 240 nm and 500 nm, with a small peak centered near 400 nm, and there is no absorbance peak between 500 and 600 nm existed. These characteristics are well matching with those previously reported GSH-AuNCs [16,22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL mechanism of the ratiometric nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, a ratiometric nanoprobe was constructed by proportionally mixing two kinds of nanoparticle solutions with different fluorescence peaks. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the fluorescence peaks of single N-CDots at 440 nm and single AuNCs at 565 nm are observed (Fig. 2a) when exciting at 380 nm. Whereas, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDots is obviously quenched about 40%, the emission of AuNCs is enhanced doubled when two kinds of nanoparticle solutions are mixed, which presumably accounts for FRET occurring.

To further confirm this FRET, the spectra overlap and Zeta potentials of the nanoparticles were studied. As can be seen from Fig. 2b, there is an overlap between the N-CDots emission spectrum and the AuNCs absorption spectrum. Zeta potentials of N-CDots and AuNCs are shown in Fig.2c. It can be clearly seen that the Zeta potential of N-CDots is +17.7 ± 1.03 mV, which attributed to large amount of amine groups on their surface. The Zeta potential of AuNCs is −6.4 ± 1.4 mV, which attributed to the fact that the surface capping agent GSH containing two carboxyl groups and an amine. After assembly, the Zeta potential of the probe is observed at −3.05 ± 1.1 mV owing to the occurrence of charge-charge interactions between the two oppositely charged nanoparticles. Therefore, the negative charges on the surface of AuNCs enable electrostatic interaction with positive charges on the surface of N-CDots. Moreover, the TEM image of N-CDots/AuNCs probe also confirmed the absorption of the two components, and their distance is close enough (Fig. 1c).
All of these conform that the N-CDots are directly connected with AuNCs by electrostatic adsorption, leading to a remarkable FRET from N-CDots to AuNCs.

In this work, we firstly examined the effect of Pb2+ and Cu2+ ions on the fluorescence signal of N-CDots and AuNCs, separately. As shown in Fig.S3-4, the addition of Pb2+ or Cu2+ represents an enhancing or quenching effect on the fluorescence of single AuNCs, while their addition to single N-CDots solution results in no measurable fluorescence change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pb2+-induced aggregation induced emission enhancement (AIEE) of AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: Unique AIEE property has been found for thiolated (e.g., GSH) AuNCs, which have an Au(0)@Au(I)-thiolate core-shell structure. Researchers have confirmed that Au(I)-thiolate complexes are nonluminescent in solution, however, exhibit strong PL upon aggregation [22,30]. The possible enhancement explanation is the so-called “ligand metal charge transfer (LMCTs)” or “ligand-to-metal-metal charge transfer (LMMCT)” mechanism as previous reported [22]. That is to say, the charge transfer might occur from the S atom in the thiolate ligands to the Au atom through Au-S bond, followed by consequent radiative relaxation. As a result, the emission of AuNCs enhanced.

Recent results suggest that Pb2+ ions could induce the aggregation of Au(I)-thiolate complexes, then enhance the emission of AuNCs [31,32]. Therefore, the ratio response of our probe to Pb2+ is attributed to the AIEE of AuNCs. To clearly verify such aggregation, the corresponding TEM image was collected (Fig. 4 a). As indicated, Pb2+ ions induce the significantly aggregation of AuNCs, the opposite charged N-CDots are surrounded the aggregated AuNCs.

It is well known that paramagnetic transition Cu2+ ions quench luminescence in general [33]. The quenching is supposedly by a charge-transfer mechanism. In other words, Cu2+ ions coordinate with the carboxyl groups of GSH formed GSH-Cu2+ complexes. Owing to the intrinsic paramagnetic properties of Cu2+, such complexes enable establish an effective charge transfer from Au to Cu2+ but block the charge-transfer of ligand-to-Au, and thereby cause the fluorescence quenching of AuNCs. By the way, the aggregation of AuNCs is occurred, as shown in Fig. 4b.

The reaction time was optimized by recording I565/I440 after the addition of 20 μM Pb2+ or Cu2+. The results are shown in Fig. S5 and Fig. S6. From the optimization data, the reaction time are all selected as 10 min. Under the optimized experimental conditions, various concentrations of Pb2+ or Cu2+ (0–100 μM) were tested using the prepared probe.

Fig. 5 a shows the fluorescence spectra of the probe in the presence of different concentrations of Pb2+. The continuous increase of fluorescence at 565 nm is observed when different doses of Pb2+ are introduced, while 440 nm shows little change. The linear calibration of Pb2+ is in the concentration range of 2–60 μM (Fig. 5b) with a regression equation of I565/I440 = 0.8275 + 0.0522C (C is the concentration of Pb2+, μM); the correlation coefficient (R) is 0.9906. Fig. 5c shows the corresponding fluorescence color change from pink to strong orange-red with the amount of Pb2+ ranging from 0 to 60 μM.

The results of the quantitative detection of Cu2+ are shown in Fig. 5a’–c’. With the addition of Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity at 565 nm of AuNCs is gradually quenched; in contrast, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm of N-CDots still stayed steady (Fig. 5a’). Accordingly, a good linear relationship between the ratio value I565/I440 and the Cu2+ concentration expanding from 1 to 60 μM is obtained (Fig. 5b’); the linear regression equation is I565/I440 = 0.8022-0.00826C’ (C’ is the concentration of Cu2+, μM) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9955. The probe fluorescence color varies from pink to blue is observed in the Fig. 5c’.

Using the 3σ rule (σ = S0/S, S0 is known as the standard deviation of the blank after repeated measurements, and S is the slope of the calibration curve), the detection limits of Pb2+ and Cu2+ were calculated to be about 0.5 μM and 0.15 μM, respectively, which are much lower than the recommended water quality standards of Pb2+ (10 μM) and Cu2+ (31.5 μM) for drinking water by World Health Organization (WHO) [34]. The detection limits of the developed probe were also compared with several previously reported fluorescent probes. The results are exhibited in Table 2 . As can be seen, the detection limits are comparable or better than most of those works [35–42]. Moreover, as a comparison, our probe has the advantage of not requiring any organic solvents.

Furthermore, the N-CDots/AuNCs probe was employed for the visual semi-quantitative determination of Pb2+ and Cu2+ in water. When the concentration of Pb2+ or Cu2+ is more than about 20 μM, the recognizable color changes of the probe could be observed by naked eyes. These results revealed that this ratiometric probe enable visual semi-quantitative detection of Pb2+ and Cu2+ in aqueous samples.

The interference of some other common metal ions such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Mg2+ was studied. The results are shown in Fig. 6 , the values remain nearly unchanged when the coexistent metal ions are added, demonstrating that the current method has certain anti-interference ability for Pb2+ and Cu2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pb2+ and Cu2+ in river water samples
ce:section.ce:para: After the investigation of linear range and selectivity performances of the ratiometric fluorescent probe, we explored its capability for detecting Pb2+ and Cu2+ in river water samples. As summarized in Table S1, the recovery ratios of the spiked sample range between 94% and 104% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 3.3%. The results demonstrated that the N-CDots/AuNCs probe provides great potential for the quantification of Pb2+ and Cu2+ in environmental samples.

In summary, we have developed a FRET ratiometric fluorescence probe based on a facile electrostatic adsorption strategy. This type of probe has such outstanding properties as simply preparation, high accuracy, high selectivity, and dual targets detection. This method also possesses the advantage of semi-quantitative detection of Pb2+ or Cu2+ by bare eyes. Whatever, the electrostatic interaction is proven to be a more convenient way to attain FRET probe than a covalent bonding process, and provides a desirable detection results for dual targets. It’s expected that our design would be beneficial for investigating FRET ratiometric sensor and dual targets during simple procedure. Nevertheless, one disadvantage of the developed strategy is there is the mutual interference when Pb2+ and Cu2+ ions coexistence. Thus, future work should be focused on improving the probe selectivity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21707053), Nature Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20140577) and Advanced Talents Science Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 10JDG052, 10JDG038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.058.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of FRET-based dual-excitation ratiometric fluorescent pH probes and their photocaged derivatives
Energy transfer cassettes based on organic fluorophores: construction and applications in ratiometric sensing
Imaging molecular interactions in living cells by FRET microscopy
Development of FRET-based ratiometric fluorescent Cu2+ chemodosimeters and the applications for living cell imaging
FRET-derived ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Cu
A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on FRET for imaging Hg2+ ions in living cells
Ratiometric sensing of mercury(II) based on a FRET process on silica; core-shell nanoparticles acting as vehicles
Unique approach to develop carbon dot-based nanohybrid near-infrared ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of mercury ions
FRET based ammonia sensor using carbon dots
Carbon dots based FRET for the detection of DNA damage
Carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting hydrogen sulfide in aqueous media and inside live cells
Dumbbell-shaped carbon quantum dots/AuNCs nanohybrid as an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing cadmium (II) ions and l-ascorbic acid
Carbon quantum dot-gold nanocluster nanosatellite for ratiometric fluorescence probe and imaging for hydrogen peroxide in living cells
Visual and ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ based on a dual-emission carbon dots-gold nanoclusters nanohybrid
The construction of a FRET assembly by using gold nanoclusters and carbon dots and their application as a ratiometric probe for cysteine detection
Enhanced fluorescence of gold nanoclusters composed of HAuCl4 and histidine by glutathione: glutathione detection and selective cancer cell imaging
Core-size dependent fluorescent gold nanoclusters and ultrasensitive detection of Pb2+ ion
Dual sensitive and selective sensor for Pb2+ and Al3+ with distinctive fluorescence response
A reaction-based long-wavelength fluorescent probe for Cu2+ detection and imaging in living cells
A FRET-based ratiometric sensor for mercury ions in water with multi-layered silica nanoparticles as the scaffold
Synthesis, separation, and characterization of small and highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots
From aggregation-induced emission of Au(I)-thiolate complexes to ultrabright Au(0)@Au(I)-thiolate core-shell nanoclusters
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on dithiothreitol modified carbon dots-gold nanoclusters for the sensitive detection of mercury ions in water samples
Surrounding media sensitive photoluminescence of boron-doped graphene quantum dots for highly fluorescent dyed crystals, chemical sensing and bioimaging
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive, label-free detection of Fe (III) in aqueous media
Chemical nature of redox-controlled photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots by post-synthesis treatment
Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications
Exploring the emissive states of heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots
An efficient on-off ratiometric fluorescence probe for cyanide ion based on perturbation of the interaction between gold nanoclusters and a copper (II)-phthalocyanine complex
A turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe for lead(II) based on the aggregation of weakly associated gold(I)-glutathione nanoparticles
Aggregation-induced accelerating peroxidase-like activity of gold nanoclusters and their applications for colorimetric Pb2+ detection
Gold nanoclusters-Cu2+ ensemble-based fluorescence turn-on and real-time assay for acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibitor screening
WHO Guidelines Values for Chemicals That Are of Health Significance in Drinking Water, Guidelines for Drinking Water Quality
A highly sensitive and selective chemosensor for Pb2+ based on quinoline–coumarin
Dual sensitive and selective sensor for Pb2+ and Al3+ with distinctive fluorescence response
Polydiacetylene-based sensor for highly sensitive and selective Pb2+ detection
A new fluorescent chemosensor for Pb2+ ions based on naphthalene derivatives
Sensitive and selective ratiometric nanosensors for visual detection of Cu2+ based on ions promoted oxidation reaction
A dual-response quinoline-based fluorescent sensor for the detection of Copper (II) and Iron(III) ions in aqueous medium
A novel solvent-dependently bifunctional NIR absorptive and fluorescent ratiometric probe for detecting Fe3+/Cu2+ and its application in bioimaging
Multi-color fluorescent carbon dots for wavelength-selective and ultrasensitive Cu2+ sensing

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Wang received her MS degree and Ph.D degree from ShaanXi Normal University. She is currently working at Department of Pharmaceutics, Jiangsu University. Her research area is development of chemosensors, aptasensors and biosensors based on application of different nanomaterials.
Hai-Xia Cao is currently doing his MS work at Department of Pharmaceutics, Jiangsu University. She is currently working toward biosensors.

Yu-Sheng He is currently doing his MS work at Department of Environment Science, Jiangsu University. His research interests include development of biosensors and advanced materials for various purposes.
Chang-Gang Pan is currently doing his MS work at Department of Environment Science, Jiangsu University. He is currently working toward functional nanomaterial.
Ting-Kai Sun is currently doing her BS work at Department of Pharmaceutics, Jiangsu University. He is currently working toward characterization of various types of nanomaterials.
Xin-Yang Zhang is currently doing her BS work at Department of Pharmaceutics, Jiangsu University. He is currently working toward development of advanced materials.
Chuan-Yun Wang is currently doing her BS work at Department of Pharmaceutics, Jiangsu University. He is currently working toward testing of advanced materials.

Guo-Xi Liang received his Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University. He is currently working at Department of Environment Science, Jiangsu University. His current research interests include nanobiosensor and nanofabrication.
